notes: Kanien'kéha distinguishes singular, dual, and plural 'you'. In Six Nations/Tyendinaga dialects í:se' becomes ý:se' (y-form).
First person pronouns. Kanien'kéha has three numbers: singular, dual (you and I), and plural (we all, inclusive). In Ontario dialects: ý:'i.
Female third-person pronouns. Kanien'kéha encodes social register directly into the pronoun: akaónha for respected females (mothers, elders); aónha for familiar females, animals, or objects.
Male third-person pronouns. Ra- marks male throughout Kanien'kéha. In Akwesasne: laónha (r→l alternation). The dual forms cover two-female (teteniiáhshe) and two-male/mixed (tehniiáhshe) pairs.
Dual 'you two'. Kanien'kéha maintains singular/dual/plural as distinct categories.
Plural 'you all'. The -kwé:kon suffix signals plurality throughout Kanien'kéha.
Singular female child. I- prefix marks female. The glottal stop (') before the final 'a is phonemically significant.
Female adolescent. The long vowel (:) is critical — shortening it changes the word.
Adult woman. Iak- prefix marks female. The root -kwe appears across Iroquoian languages, related to personhood.
Elder woman — not merely 'old woman'. In Kanien'kehá:ka matrilineal society, elder women hold central ceremonial and social authority.
Singular male child. R- marks male. In Akwesasne: Laksá:'a.
Male adolescent. Ra- prefix marks male. In Akwesasne: Lanekénhteron.
Adult male. Compare Iakón:kwe (woman) — same -kwe root, different gender prefix. In Akwesasne: Lón:kwe.
Elder male. Same -stén:ha suffix as Akokstén:ha (elder woman). In Akwesasne: Lokstén:ha.
Maternal kinship term. Kanien'kehá:ka society is matrilineal — clan membership passes through the mother's line. Possessive prefixes change the entire word.
Paternal kinship term. The possessive prefix changes the entire word form.
Grandfather — a term of deep respect. The glottal stop begins the word.
Grandmother. Featured in the Kontinónhstats animated video 'Beading with Tota'. Elder women hold central ceremonial authority.
Your older sister. Kinship terms encode the possessor and the relative age of siblings — English 'sister' carries none of this information.
Your older brother. The Ia-/Ra- prefix alternation (female/male possessor) is consistent across the kinship system.
Kinship nouns are always possessed in Kanien'kéha. Two dialect forms: Kanehsatà:ke/Kahnawà:ke uses the -ksen'a root; Six Nations uses the -yén:'a root.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats L5 · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke; eCampusOntario OER (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Kinship nouns in Kanien'kéha are always possessed — the prefix changes to mark whose son is being named. Two dialect forms: Kanehsatà:ke/Kahnawà:ke uses the -ksen'a root; Six Nations uses the -yén:'a root.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats L5 · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke; eCampusOntario OER (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Response to 'where are you going?'. wà:ke = I am going. Used with a destination: 'Ake'nistén:ha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke' (I am going to my mother's house).

A-class noun (begins with 'a'). The verb incorporation example: áhta' + -owá:nen → watahkowá:nen (a big shoe). This demonstrates noun incorporation, one of Kanien'kéha's most distinctive grammatical features.
A-class noun. The at- prefix is common in A-class nouns and often indicates something worn on the body.
A 'fake' A-class noun — the root meaning 'body cover' is still identifiable. When you can describe the root meaning it usually derives from a verb.
A-class noun for earrings. The ate'- prefix appears in several items worn or attached to the body.
A-class noun for glasses. The atk- cluster is characteristic of several A-class body-related nouns.
A-class noun for chair. Household and everyday object vocabulary.
A-class noun for ball. Note the long vowel (:) before the final syllable.
A 'true' A-class noun — the original root meaning has been lost over time, so only the word survives. This is described in the Kontinónhstats curriculum as an example of how language evolves.
A-stem verb. Command: s- (you) + á:tien. Habitual: k- (I) + á:tien + -s. Dual and plural command forms use the seni-/sewa- prefixes.
Dualic A-stem verb. The te- dualic prefix is obligatory — it cannot be dropped. Describes a two-phase motion: stopping then moving forward.
A-stem verb with the at- middle voice infix, common in body-related and reflexive verbs. Habitual suffix -s marks repeated action.
The definite article 'the' in Kanien'kéha. 'ne' marks a specific, identifiable noun. Unlike English, Kanien'kéha articles also signal animacy and social register in more complex sentences.

cite.text: Mithun (1982); Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words; Kontinónhstats curriculum structure

notes: Used before nouns in directional contexts — 'the' when going to get something. From the Lesson 1 example: 'Wá:s skó:ha thi áhta' (Go get the shoe).

cite.text: Kontinónhstats L1 example · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

notes: O-class noun beginning with Ó. Glass and ice share this word — the connection between transparency and cold is embedded in the language.
O-class noun for necklace. Traditional beadwork and wampum are central to Kanien'kehá:ka cultural practice.

O-class noun for money. Ohwísta is mentioned in the Kontinónhstats curriculum opening — 'seeking funding' is expressed using this word. It reflects how Kanien'kéha has adapted to modern economic concepts.
O-class noun for sugar or candy. This word appears in the Lesson 1 curriculum example sentence: 'Shní:non thi otsikhè:ta' (You buy the candy).

cite.text: Kontinónhstats L8 & L1 · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke

notes: O-class noun for water. Water is one of the first beings acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address — 'Onkwehshón:'a ne wáhi ne Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen' opens with gratitude to the waters.
O-class noun for feather. Feathers carry ceremonial significance in Haudenosaunee culture — eagle feathers in particular.
O-class noun for flower. Also appears in the Thanksgiving Address — the flowers of the earth are acknowledged and thanked.
O-class noun for leaf. Part of the nature vocabulary that connects language to the land.
Ka-class noun for car. A loanword concept — Kanien'kéha adapted the noun class system to name modern objects. The Ka- prefix marks this class.

Ka-class noun for paper or book. The root -hiatónhsera is related to writing — kahiatónhsera is literally 'writing-thing'. This is a good example of how Kanien'kéha forms nouns by describing function.
Ka-class noun for door. One of the household vocabulary items in Mary McDonald's curriculum.
Ka-class noun for boat. Watercraft is historically significant for Kanien'kehá:ka people whose territory includes the St. Lawrence River and Lake of Two Mountains.
Ka-class noun for drum. The drum is central to Haudenosaunee ceremony and social dance. Knowing this word connects language to cultural practice.
Ka-class noun for flint or arrowhead. The Haudenosaunee confederacy — the 'People of the Flint' — takes its name from flint. Kanien'kehá:ka means 'People of the Place of Flint'.

The name of the community — often anglicised as 'Oka'. Kanehsatà:ke means 'where the crust of snow on top of the earth is'. The 1990 Oka Crisis centred on this community's defence of their land, including a sacred pine grove. The community's name in its own language carries this history.
The sun — also used in Kanehsatà:ke place and directional vocabulary. The sun appears in the Thanksgiving Address as one of the beings to be thanked.

english: down the bay / Toward the water
notes: Directional term used in Kanehsatà:ke geography — 'toward the bay' or 'down toward the water'. Place knowledge is encoded in the language.
sortKey: down the bay / toward the water

notes: Red. Colour nouns in Kanien'kéha are often O-class nouns. Onekwénhtara is related to the word for blood — the colour name is descriptive of what is red.
Blue. Oròn:ia is related to the word for sky — blue is the colour of what is above.
Green. Óhonte' is related to the word for grass or vegetation — green is the colour of what grows.
Yellow. The long vowel (:) in Otsì:nekwahr is significant.
Black. One of the basic colour terms.
White. Compare with Ó:wise' (glass/ice) — there may be a shared root related to transparency or paleness.
Brown. The colour of earth and bark.
Pink.
Head. Body part vocabulary in Kanien'kéha is important for learning the middle-voice infix -at- that appears in reflexive verbs (washing one's face, brushing one's teeth, etc.).
Hair — literally 'on the head'. Many body part words in Kanien'kéha are compound forms showing location.
Eye. The root -nen appears in verb forms related to seeing.
Nose. The -yén:ta root is related to smelling and appears in verb forms.
Lips. Connected to speaking and eating verb roots.
Teeth. Appears in the verb 'to brush one's teeth' from the A-stem lesson: satkahnekáhrhons.
Hand. The root -nhstà:'a is incorporated into the verb 'to wash one's hands' from the A-stem verbs: sato'nnenhstáhkwa's.
Arm.
One. Numbers in Kanien'kéha are used for counting, telling time, and expressing quantities. Note that Énska is also the root seen in the word for 'one at a time'.
Two. You have already seen this root in the dual prefix tékeni- (two people). The connection is explicit — duality is built on the number two.
Three. Plural person nouns (three or more) are named relative to this number — the plural threshold.
Four. Also a common root in place names and time expressions.
Five. Wísk is a short, sharp word. Numbers 1–10 form the basis for all counting in Kanien'kéha.
Six. The Haudenosaunee confederacy is made up of Six Nations — Ia:ia'k Onkwehonwe ('six peoples').
Seven.
Eight. The glottal stop (') in sha'té:kon is part of the word — do not drop it.
Nine.
Ten. From ten onwards, numbers are formed by combining roots: eleven = ten + one (óia'k énska), twenty = two tens (tékeni óia'k), and so on.

english: thanksgiving Address (lit. 'the words that come before all else')
notes: The Haudenosaunee Thanksgiving Address — the most important oral text in Kanien'kéha. Recited at the opening and closing of all gatherings, it acknowledges every element of creation in order. The Kanehsatà:ke dialect version is the basis for the animated film produced by Kontinónhstats.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats animated film · Kanehsatà:ke community; NMAI PDF

sortKey: thanksgiving address (lit. 'the words that come before all else')

notes: 'The people' or 'all human beings' — the first group acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address. Onkwehshón:'a = original/real people.
Star — also appears in the night sky vocabulary of the Thanksgiving Address. The stars are the last beings acknowledged before the Creator.
Rectangle — Ka-class shape noun.
Square — the te- prefix is the dualic prefix you already know, here marking equal sides.
Oval — Ka-class shape noun.
Triangle — literally 'three sides'. You already know Áhsen (three) from the numbers lesson. Shape names in Kanien'kéha describe the shape's properties, not just name it.
Circle — te- prefix + noun root. The circle is a sacred shape in Haudenosaunee tradition, representing the continuous cycle of life.
Diamond — literally 'four sides'. Kaié:ri (four) + nikahióhsake (sides). Another numerically-descriptive shape name.
Sun — acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address as 'our eldest brother who carries his face across the sky each day'. You first encountered this word in the Kanehsatà:ke place names lesson.

Bear — one of the three Mohawk clan animals. The Bear Clan (Rotihskeré:wake) is one of the three clans of the Kanien'kehá:ka. The bear appears in the Tóta tánon Ohkwá:ri puppet show produced in Kahnawà:ke.
Turtle — one of the three Mohawk clan animals. The Turtle Clan (Rotiniáhton). In Haudenosaunee creation story, Turtle Island (North America) is formed on the back of a great turtle.
Wolf — one of the three Mohawk clan animals. The Wolf Clan (Ronathahí:non). The three original Mohawk clans are Bear, Turtle, and Wolf.
Beaver. Historically important as a trade animal — the beaver pelt trade dramatically affected Kanien'kehá:ka life and politics in the 17th and 18th centuries.
Wild rabbit. A common animal in the Kanehsatà:ke region.
Deer. Venison is a traditional food; deer hides were used for clothing. The deer appears in clan and ceremony vocabulary.
Moose — the largest land animal in the Kanehsatà:ke territory. The St. Lawrence lowlands and Laurentians have been moose territory for Kanien'kehá:ka hunters.
Porcupine. Porcupine quills are used in traditional quillwork — an art form predating European beads.
Cat. Domestic animals arrived with European contact — their names in Kanien'kéha were created using the language's own word-formation rules, not borrowed directly.
Horse — literally related to 'it carries on its back'. The horse arrived with European contact in the 17th century; the word describes the animal's function rather than borrowing a foreign name.
Cow. Another post-contact domesticated animal. Compare Tiohnhónhskwaron rá:tsi'n (bull) — the bull is named as the male form of the cow.
Goat. Ka-class domestic animal noun.
Sheep — literally 'it has wool on its back'. Descriptive compound: the animal is named for its defining characteristic. This is Kanien'kéha's productive word-formation at work.

Bear Clan — one of the three original Mohawk clans. Clan membership is matrilineal: you belong to your mother's clan. The Bear Clan is known in oral tradition for its strength and its role as healers.
Turtle Clan — one of the three original Mohawk clans. The Turtle Clan is associated with leadership and is connected to Turtle Island, the Haudenosaunee name for North America.
Wolf Clan — one of the three original Mohawk clans. The Wolf Clan is associated with pathfinding and trail-keeping — those who know the way.

Snipe Clan — originally an Onondaga clan present in Kanehsatà:ke due to the historical resettlement of Oswegatchie Onondaga people by 1806. The presence of non-Mohawk clans at Kanehsatà:ke is part of the community's specific history.
Eel Clan — also originally Onondaga, present at Kanehsatà:ke through the same historical resettlement. The Eel Clan's presence at Kanehsatà:ke reflects the community's unique composition.
Strawberries — one of the first foods of the spring season, celebrated in the Strawberry Ceremony. Strawberries hold a special place in Haudenosaunee ceremonial life as the first fruit of the year.
Cranberries — a traditional food of the Kanehsatà:ke region, found in the bogs and wet areas near the community.
Raspberries — a summer fruit gathered in the forests and clearings of the Kanehsatà:ke area.
Cherries. The cherry tree is also part of traditional medicine knowledge.
Blueberries — abundant in the region and important in traditional diet. Blueberries are gathered in late summer.

Apples. Apple orchards were significant at Kanehsatà:ke — the 1990 Oka Crisis was partly triggered by a golf course development that would have destroyed orchards and a sacred pine grove on traditional territory.
Potatoes. An important staple food in modern Kanien'kehá:ka households.
Meat — general term. Wild game (deer, moose, rabbit) has been central to Kanien'kehá:ka diet alongside the Three Sisters.
Butter — a post-contact food item, named using Kanien'kéha word-formation principles rather than borrowed directly.

Beans — one of the Three Sisters (corn, beans, squash), the foundation crops of Haudenosaunee agriculture. The Three Sisters are companion plants: corn provides the trellis, beans fix nitrogen, squash shades the ground.
Corn — the most sacred of the Three Sisters. Corn is central to Haudenosaunee ceremony, agriculture, and creation stories. The word appears in compound forms throughout the vocabulary.
Squash — the third of the Three Sisters. Together, corn, beans, and squash (Onón:ta, Ohsahéta', Otá:ra) form the agricultural and ceremonial cornerstone of Haudenosaunee culture.
Bread. Corn bread (onón:ta kanà:taros) is a traditional food.
Bear meat — a compound noun: Ohkwá:ri (bear) + o'wá:ron (meat). This demonstrates how nouns combine in Kanien'kéha. You already know both component words.

Longhouse — the traditional communal dwelling and the social/political unit of Haudenosaunee life. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy is called 'People of the Longhouse'. Clan mothers governed the longhouse.

english: the Great Law of Peace / The Good Path
notes: The Great Law of Peace — the constitution of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, brought by the Peacemaker. Kaià:ner means 'good path' or 'good way'. Haudenosaunee political philosophy expressed in this law influenced the US Constitution according to some historians.
sortKey: the great law of peace / the good path

notes: Wampum belt — teioháte. Wampum belts are records of treaties, alliances, and oral agreements. The Kanehsatà:ke Two Dog Wampum Belt was returned from the McCord Stewart Museum in 2024 — a major act of cultural repatriation. See the news item on the Kontinónhstats website.

Peace — central to Haudenosaunee identity. The Peacemaker (Tekanawí:ta) brought Skennón:'a, Kasastensera (strength/righteousness), and Kanikonhri:io (good mind) as the three principles of the Great Law.
Native tobacco — used ceremonially, not recreationally. Tobacco carries prayers upward to the Creator. It is placed in fire, water, or the earth as an offering and thanks.

Corn husk doll — a traditional toy and cultural object made from dried corn husks. According to Haudenosaunee tradition, the corn husk doll has no face — she gave her face to the people and was taught humility.
Soap. Household vocabulary shows how Kanien'kéha has adapted to modern domestic life through its own word-formation system.
Bed — literally related to 'where one rests'. The -yentha' suffix is a locative indicating the place of the action.
Towel or face cloth. Body-care vocabulary connects to the A-stem verbs you learned (washing face, brushing teeth).
Moon — acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address as our Grandmother Moon who governs the tides and the female cycle. The moon is feminine in Haudenosaunee tradition.
Earth — the same root you saw in 'flower' (Ohwén:tsia). The earth and what grows from it share a root — the language encodes their relationship.
Maple — the tree of the Kanehsatà:ke region and a cornerstone of the sugar bush tradition. Maple sap (ohsawén:ton wahta) is gathered each spring in a ceremony connecting the community to the land.
River — literally 'on the river' or 'by the water'. The St. Lawrence River (Kaniatarà:ke) is central to Kanehsatà:ke geography and history. The Lake of Two Mountains is nearby.
Mountains — literally 'where they are tall'. The Blue Mountain (Karáhkwa, in local usage) is visible from Kanehsatà:ke and is part of the community's geographic identity.
Winter. The four seasons in Kanien'kéha reflect the agricultural and ceremonial calendar of Haudenosaunee life.
Spring — the season of renewal, first foods ceremonies, and the return of the maple sap. The Strawberry Ceremony follows shortly after.
Summer. The growing season — corn, beans, and squash are tended through the summer months.
Autumn — the harvest season. The Green Corn Ceremony and Harvest Ceremony mark this time of year.

The Peacemaker — the founder of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy who brought together five warring nations under the Great Law of Peace. He is not named directly in many Haudenosaunee communities out of respect; here we use the respectful form Tekanawí:ta.

Hiawatha (Aiionwátha) — the Mohawk chief who worked alongside the Peacemaker to unite the nations. Not to be confused with Longfellow's fictional 'Hiawatha' — the historical Aiionwátha is a Haudenosaunee political figure of immense importance.

Longhouse Chief — a male chief selected by the clan mother of his clan. Chiefs serve at the pleasure of the clan mothers and can be removed ('dehorned') if they do not serve the people well. This selection system is part of the Great Law.

Clan Mother — the most important political figure in the Haudenosaunee system. Clan mothers select and remove chiefs, hold clan membership records, and are the keepers of the lineage. The language and the political system speak together here.

The Creator — acknowledged last in the Thanksgiving Address. Shonkwaiá:tison = 'the one who created us'. The Creator is named at the very end of the Address, after all of creation has been acknowledged.
Singers — Ratiwennahní:ion. Song and ceremony are inseparable in Haudenosaunee tradition. Social dances, condolence ceremonies, and ceremonial cycles all have distinct songs.
Hammer — a 'famine indefinite' noun beginning with Ie-. These verb-derived nouns describe what the object does: the hammer-thing.
Shovel or spade — another Ie- class tool noun derived from a verb root meaning 'to dig'.
Scissors — Ion- class noun, verb-derived: 'the thing that cuts things'. Note how the word describes the function.
Knife — Iet- class tool noun, verb-derived from a cutting root.
Awl or hole puncher — Iak- class tool noun. Traditional awls were used to punch holes in hides for sewing.
Screwdriver — modern tool named using the -hon:ni pattern (the thing that does X). The language generates names for new tools from existing verb roots.
Consonant-stem verb. The root begins with k-, so the first-person habitual doubles the initial consonant: k- + kwe'... → kkwe'tarón:kwas.
Dualic consonant-stem verb command. Structure: te- (dualic) + ts- (you) + hon'ká:ria'k (consonant root). Sawing requires two motions: push and pull — hence dualic.
Consonant-stem command: s- + káhere'. Dual and plural forms use the áhseron root variant.
You give — consonant-stem command. Used in everyday exchanges and in ceremonial contexts.

I-stem verb command form. The I-stem is distinctive because the pronoun 'you' appears as ts- rather than s-. The verb root begins with i (or a sound that triggers the ts- pronoun). This class also contains a distinctive (sh) sound between consonants.
Dualic I-stem habitual. Bricklaying is dualic — you place a brick (one motion) and set it in mortar (second motion). The -rhó:tha' habitual suffix marks this as an ongoing occupation.
Making fire — an I-stem command verb. The ability to make fire is a fundamental skill referenced throughout Haudenosaunee oral tradition.
True E-stem verb — the verb root begins with é. Structure: s- (you) + é:ta (root). Commands never have a suffix.
Remember — True E-stem question/command form. Used in both questions ('Do you remember?') and affirmations.
You want — True E-stem verb expressing desire or want. Central to daily conversation.
Mowing the lawn — E-stem command. A modern activity named using the language's existing verb-stem system.
Trout — the general word for fish in some contexts. Trout were abundant in the rivers and streams of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.
Sturgeon — a large, ancient fish of the St. Lawrence River. Lake sturgeon were a major food source and are now endangered due to overfishing and habitat loss.
Pike — a predatory fish of the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence watershed. Note the similarity to Ohkwá:ri (bear) — both begin with ohkwa-, though they are distinct words.
Salmon — once abundant in the St. Lawrence, Atlantic salmon runs were dramatically reduced by dam construction and pollution. Their return is part of river restoration efforts.

Eel — the Eel Clan (Rotineniotthró:non) of Kanehsatà:ke takes its name from this fish. American eels migrate through the St. Lawrence; their populations have collapsed due to dams and pollution. Eel restoration is an environmental justice issue in Haudenosaunee territory.
Bass — smallmouth and largemouth bass are abundant in the Lake of Two Mountains near Kanehsatà:ke.
Sunfish — common in the shallow waters of the lakes and rivers around Kanehsatà:ke.

Eagle — the eagle carries prayers upward to the Creator. Eagle feathers hold deep ceremonial significance in Haudenosaunee tradition. The eagle perches at the top of the Great Tree of Peace in the creation story of the Confederacy.
Robin — one of the first birds to return in spring, signalling the end of winter. The robin's arrival is part of the seasonal awareness encoded in Haudenosaunee ceremonial life.
Hawk — a hunting bird of the Kanehsatà:ke territory. The hawk is acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address among the birds.
Great Horned Owl — in some Haudenosaunee traditions, the owl is associated with death or medicine. The owl's call at night carries significance beyond its literal sound.
Owl (general) — distinct from the Horned Owl. The owl's night vision and silent flight have long made it significant in oral tradition.
Goose — Canada geese (Káhonk) are abundant at Kanehsatà:ke. Their honking call in spring and autumn marks the changing of seasons as reliably as the calendar.

Loon — the loon's call on the lake is one of the distinctive sounds of the Kanehsatà:ke territory. The loon dives deep — in some traditions it is associated with persistence and the ability to navigate between worlds.

Heron — the heron's patience and precision in fishing make it a figure of stillness and skill in oral tradition. The heron stands motionless at the river's edge — a quality valued in hunters and healers alike.

Eleven = ten + one. Óia'k (ten) + énska (one). All numbers above ten in Kanien'kéha are compound — built from the ten-base plus the units. This base-10 additive system is transparent and logical once you know 1–10.
Twelve = ten + two. The number system is completely transparent: óia'k (10) + tékeni (2) = 12. No irregular forms to memorise.
Twenty = two tens. Tékeni (two) + óia'k (ten). Note the order reverses: for 11–19, ten comes first (óia'k énska). For multiples of ten (20, 30…), the multiplier comes first (tékeni óia'k).
Fifty = five tens. Wísk (five) + óia'k (ten). The pattern is consistent: once you know 1–10, you can construct any number to 100.
One hundred — a single word rather than a compound. Tewá:sen is the base for 100; larger hundreds are formed by multiplying (tékeni tewá:sen = 200, áhsen tewá:sen = 300, etc.).

O/On-stem verb: the root begins with 'o'. sohkwá:ren = be strong (command). Structure: s- (you) + ohkwá:ren (O-stem root meaning strength/being strong). The O-stem is one of the six verb classes in Mary McDonald's curriculum.
O-stem verb meaning to be cold — used for weather ('it is cold') and bodily sensation. satonhnhéton = be cold. A practical weather and sensation verb.
O-stem verb for being sick or ill. saiosé:ren = be sick (command/question form). Essential health vocabulary.
O-stem verb for looking or seeing. satkatho = look (command). A common action verb also used in the 'what do you see?' dialogue pattern.

Fake E-stem verb: the root looks like it starts with a vowel but is grammatically a consonant-stem class. sahrón:ni = make it (command). One of the most productive verbs in Kanien'kéha — everything you make uses this root.
Fake E-stem verb for carrying. sahsénhawe = carry it (command). Connects to the naming of horse (Akohsá:tens = 'it carries on its back') — the same -hawe root.

Fake E-stem verb for spending money. Ohwísta (money) you already know — this verb incorporates the money root: sahwista- + -hní:ios (habitual). A real-world modern verb formed entirely from Kanien'kéha roots.

It is cold — weather expression using the O-stem verb root -tonhnhéton. In Kanien'kéha, weather is expressed as a verb ('it is cold-ing'), not as an adjective ('the weather is cold'). This reflects a language that encodes process rather than state.
It is raining — O-stem weather verb. Short, crisp, impossible to confuse. Rain (ó:iats) and water (Ónhka) share no surface similarity — Kanien'kéha distinguishes the weather event from the substance.
It is snowing. The root -né:ka' is also found in words for ice and snow-covered ground. Kenién:ke (winter) brings ohné:ka' — the two words belong to the same seasonal vocabulary cluster.
It is warm — the weather verb corresponding to the O-stem 'strong/warm'. Compare sohkwá:ren (be strong — command form of the same root used for warmth and strength).
Big / large — the qualifier suffix you saw in noun incorporation: áhta' + -owá:nen → watahkowá:nen (a big shoe). As a standalone qualifier, owá:nen can follow O-class nouns and verbs to intensify.

Good / nice / beautiful — one of the most important qualifiers in Kanien'kéha. Ohsì:io describes people, objects, weather, and actions. Related to the root in Kanikonhri:io (good mind) — one of the three principles of the Great Law of Peace.

Calm / peaceful / quiet. Skén:nen is related to Skennón:'a (peace) — the same root appears in the peace vocabulary from the Great Law of Peace unit. 'Skén:nen kón:we?' is a traditional greeting meaning 'are you in peace / are you well?'
And / also — the most common connector in Kanien'kéha sentences. 'Rón:kwe tánon Iakón:kwe' = a man and a woman. You've already seen this particle in the Kontinónhstats organizational text.

Locative particle — where, at, when. You've seen tsi in direction sentences: 'Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke'' (I am going to where my grandmother's house is). Tsi marks location or time relative to an action.
Just / only / already. You've seen ki' in the ceremonial closing Onen' ki' wáhi (that's it now, truly). Ki' modifies verbs and nouns to indicate recency, limitation, or completeness.
Now / then / at this point. Also seen in Onen' ki' wáhi. Onen' marks a transition in time or topic — 'now (we move to)...', 'at this point...'. Central to connected discourse.

notes: Younger sister (addressed to someone). Kanien'kéha distinguishes older and younger siblings — the relative age is grammatically encoded. Compare Sahtsí:'a (your older sister).

notes: Younger brother (addressed to someone). The younger-sibling terms encode age relative to the speaker's clan position.

cite.text: Kontinónhstats · Mary McDonald, Kanehsatà:ke; eCampusOntario OER (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Sister-in-law. In-law kinship terms reflect the clan-based marriage system — your sister-in-law is necessarily from a different clan.

Male friend / boyfriend. The Kontinónhstats L5 curriculum includes friendship terms alongside formal kinship — reflecting that social relationships in Kanien'kéha are as grammatically structured as biological ones.
Granddaughter. The grandchild terms in Kanien'kéha encode gender, continuing the systematic gender-marking through the full kinship system.
Log or wooden blocks. O-class noun — okará:skwa, related to wood and trees.
Pencil or pen — oká:ra. A post-contact object given an O-class name. The word is related to 'marking' or 'drawing'.
Rattle — okwéskons. The rattle is a ceremonial instrument central to Haudenosaunee social dances and healing ceremonies.
Blouse or top — owiská:ions. An O-class clothing noun.
Skirt — oniánhsa'. O-class clothing noun.
Light or lamp — onóhkwa. One of the O-class nouns for illumination objects.
Window — owisthó:kon. A post-contact architectural feature given a descriptive O-class name.
Paint — ohwéntsya. Note this word shares a root with Ohwén:tsia (earth/flower) — both relate to what is applied to or grows from a surface.
Butter — oskenón:ton. A post-contact food item given an O-class name.
Hominy corn — onennó:ton. A processed corn product central to Haudenosaunee food preparation. The corn is treated with lye (an alkaline process called nixtamalization) before cooking.
Leather — otsi'ten:ra. O-class material noun. Traditionally deer hide was the primary leather material.
Nuts — onhkwa'shón:'a. Traditional food gathered in autumn. Hickory nuts, walnuts, and butternuts were harvested in the Kanehsatà:ke region.
Fur — related to the leather and feather vocabulary. Fur trade was historically transformative for Kanien'kehá:ka people.
Root or roots — oterón:ta. Plant roots are part of traditional medicine knowledge. The root of the word is related to going into the ground.
Oar — onhkwarón:ton. Used with the canoe (Kaná:tsihs) you already know. The oar is how one moves through the water that is Kanehsatà:ke's defining geography.
Pine tree — ohnhó:ten. The Great Tree of Peace (the white pine) is the symbol of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy. The 1990 Oka Crisis centred on the defence of a sacred pine grove at Kanehsatà:ke.

Colour — otsikhé:ta. Wait — you know otsikhè:ta as 'sugar/candy'. These are distinct words; colour is otsikhé:ta (different accent pattern). The language distinguishes them by stress and vowel quality.
Dresser, cupboard, or coffin — karón:to. Ka-class noun. The range of meanings (storage furniture and coffin) reflects a shared feature: a box-like container that holds things.
Nail or needle — note: this is distinct from Kaná:tsihs (boat/canoe) which has a different stress pattern. This entry is for the piercing implement.
Bag or sack — karahkwénies. Ka-class noun for a carrying container.
Clock — karihtón:ni. A post-contact timekeeping device given a Ka-class descriptive name. The root suggests 'the thing that marks/measures'.
Purse — kahnonhsatón:ni. Ka-class noun.
Snowshoes — kanonhkwá:tons. Traditional snowshoes were essential winter travel technology in the Kanehsatà:ke region. The Ka- prefix identifies this as a Ka-class noun.
Socks or stockings — kahsen:ón:ni.
Key — kahionhá:ton. Ka-class noun. The key shares the Ka- class with the door (kanonhsatón:ni) it opens.
Cradleboard — traditionally used to carry infants. A central object of early childhood in Haudenosaunee culture.
Pottery, can, or bottle. Pre-contact: pottery vessels. Post-contact: cans and bottles. Same word, different materials across history.
Who — Ónhka. You have already seen this as a question word in the dialog patterns (Ónhka ní:se'? = who are you?). Now it appears as part of the systematic particle inventory.
What — Nahò:ten. The question word you know from Nahò:ten katkáhthos? (what do you see?). Also appears in various compound question forms.
Where — Ka'. You know this from the direction verb questions (Ka' wáhse'? = where are you going?). A fundamental spatial question word.
When — literally 'what time is it?' used as a temporal question word. The same niiohtonhátie root you know from time-telling.
Why — literally 'what is the reason/before it'. ohén:ton (before / the cause of) you know from Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen (the words before all else).
Hurry, quickly — tióhskon. A manner particle used to urge speed.
It is cold — ionkwahró:ri. Weather state particle. Connects to the weather vocabulary in Unit 14.
It is hot — iotontátsion. The complement to ionkwahró:ri (cold).
Cold water — ohnhó:kon. Compound: cold + water. You know ónhka (water); the cold modifier creates this compound.
Hot water — ionnhóhton. Compound: hot + water.
Fast — ioia'táwen. The state of moving quickly.
Slow — iontá:wen. The complement to fast.
Good — ioiá:ner (variant). The quality of goodness appears in many compound expressions.
Bad or ugly — ioia'ko:wa. The negative quality particle.
Long ago — sewathahkwení:io. The standard story-opening temporal marker. Related to Sewatkwenhtó:ke (the storytelling opener).
Wet — oiónni. Physical state particle.
Dry — ioskaié:ri. The complement to wet.
Different — tewatkaráhton. The te- dualic prefix marks the 'two-ness' of difference — something is different from something else.
Big or large — owá:nen. The standalone size particle. Compare with the noun augmentative suffix -owá:nen (big shoe) — same root, different grammatical role.
Small — the diminutive state particle. Compare with -'a diminutive suffix on nouns.
Yes — hen'. The standard affirmative particle in Kanien'kéha. Compare with Tho niioht! (that's right) for a stronger affirmation.
No / not — Iáh. You already know this as the negation particle. Here it appears in its full system context as part of the particle inventory. Iáh alone = no; Iáh + verb = verb negation.
Never — Iáh (not) + ohnwén:ton (always/ever) = never. Compositional negation: same Iáh you know, combined with a time particle.
Heavy — okwáhson. Physical weight state.
Light in weight — iokwénton. Not light as in illumination (onóhkwa) but light as in not heavy.
Dirty — iotia'tó:ren. The state of being soiled.
It is old — iohskatá:ien. Used of objects. Compare with Rokstén:ha (old man) — age is expressed differently for people vs objects.
It smells good — iatewátkon. Sensory quality particle. Connects to smell verbs and the natural world vocabulary.
It stinks — ioriwá:nente'. The negative sensory complement.
Full or stuffed — ionkwahserón:ni. Used for containers, stomachs, spaces.
Open — iohwéntsia'te'. The state of being open (door, container, wound).
Closed — iotkahrón:ni. The complement to open.
A-stem dressing verb. s- (you) + énha (root). Command form only in source material.
Brush your teeth — A-stem habitual incorporating the tooth root -nekáhrha- (you already know onékahrha = teeth). This is noun incorporation in action: one word = brush + teeth + you + habitual.
Wash your hands — incorporating the hand root -nhstà:'a- (onenhstà:'a = hand). The same root you know from body parts.
A-stem verb. Command: s- + átskhwa'. Dual and plural command forms use seni-/sewa- prefixes.
A-stem motion verb. Habitual: s- (you) + aié:re + -s. One of the most common motion verbs in daily conversation.
A-stem verb for sleep. A fundamental daily routine verb.
A-stem study/read verb. Habitual suffix -s and command form without suffix. A key educational verb.
Cough — A-stem command. A body function verb.
Button up or fasten — A-stem habitual. Used for clothing fastening.
Hide — dualic A-stem command. te- (dualic) + sa- (you) + 'nikónhraien (concealment root). Hiding involves two states: being visible and becoming hidden.
A-stem wipe verb. Note the object incorporation differs between singular (table root) and dual/plural (window root) forms in the source material.
Blow your nose — A-stem command incorporating the nose root. You know oyén:ta (nose) from the body parts lesson.
Slide — dualic A-stem command. te- (dualic — the motion of sliding has two phases) + s- + ont (root).
Make bread or pie — fake E-stem command. A food-preparation verb.
Peel — fake E-stem command. Used for peeling vegetables and fruit.
Choose — fake E-stem habitual.
Shuck corn — fake E-stem command. A corn-processing verb central to traditional food preparation. You know onón:ta (corn).
Iron (clothing) — fake E-stem command. A post-contact household task named using existing verb morphology.
Cook meat — fake E-stem command incorporating the meat root. You know o'wá:ron (meat).
Chill or cool — fake E-stem command.
Hang up — fake E-stem command.
Freeze — fake E-stem command.
Buy a car — fake E-stem command. The car (Kà:sere') you know from Ka-class nouns appears here as the incorporated noun root in this verb.
Dry the dishes — fake E-stem command.
Dance — dualic fake E-stem command. te- (dualic — dancing involves two rhythmic phases) + s- (you) + enón:niahkhw (dance root). Social dances are central to Haudenosaunee community life.
Sing — fake E-stem command. You know ratiwennahní:ion (singers) from the cultural vocabulary. Now you have the verb to sing.
Blow up — fake E-stem command.

english: you put objects next to each other (command)
notes: Put objects next to each other — fake E-stem command.
sortKey: you put objects next to each other (command)

notes: Pry up — fake E-stem command.
The village — the central area of Kanehsatà:ke. Ohnikaieron'tò:ten is the Kanien'kéha name for the main settled area of the community.
The lacrosse arena or box at Kanehsatà:ke — otsísto (star / arena). Lacrosse (tewaarathon) is the Creator's game and central to community life.
In the pines — the sacred pine grove area of Kanehsatà:ke that was at the centre of the 1990 Oka Crisis.
Oka Park / up the bay area — Kakahkwén:tes. The bay and shoreline area of the community.
Center Road — Kaié:rinikahióhsake, the main road through Kanehsatà:ke. The name contains kaié:ri (four) — possibly 'four-sided' or 'the fourth road'.
Blue Mountain — Karákwa. The mountain visible from Kanehsatà:ke, part of the community's geographic identity. Note: distinct from Karáhkwa (sun) — different accent.
Arrow — kaién:kwire'. A shape from Lesson 20. The arrow is also a traditional weapon/hunting tool; its shape name reflects the pointed directional form.
Cloud — onhkwatón:'a. Appears both as a shape (Lesson 20) and as a nature noun. Clouds are acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address.
Heart — the heart shape. Related to nikonhraién:ta (mind/heart) — in Kanien'kéha, the heart and mind are closely linked conceptually.
Half moon — tsi ioteniáton. Both a shape and a phase of Tsí:tewen (Grandmother Moon). The half moon marks specific points in the lunar cycle relevant to ceremony and planting.
Grey — oseráhne. The eleventh colour in Mary McDonald's Lesson 21 curriculum.
Orange — otiarèn:ta. From Lesson 21. The colour of autumn leaves and the flame of the ceremonial fire.
Purple — ariwa'konhné:ha. From Lesson 21. Wampum beads come in two colours: white and purple (the latter from quahog shells). Purple wampum held higher ceremonial significance.
Skunk — shikó:hs. A forest animal of the Kanehsatà:ke region.
Snake — o'nó:wara. Snakes appear in oral tradition — the great water serpent (from Lesson 34 cultural heroes) is a supernatural version of the snake.
Fox — tsinón:we. A clever forest animal in Haudenosaunee oral tradition.

Muskrat — tanón:kereh. An aquatic animal of the wetlands near Kanehsatà:ke. In some Haudenosaunee versions of the creation story, it is the muskrat who succeeds in diving to the bottom of the sea to bring up the earth.
Elk — ohskénhton. Once abundant in the region; now rarely seen in the area.
Buffalo — tiotié:ren. Although bison no longer range in the Kanehsatà:ke territory, they were important to western Haudenosaunee trade networks and oral tradition.
Bobcat — tsi otiié:rake. A small wild cat of the forested areas.
Mink — a small semi-aquatic animal whose fur was significant in trade. The fur trade vocabulary connects to the mink's role in Haudenosaunee–European contact.
Otter — enia'tarón:nis. An aquatic mammal of the rivers and lakes near Kanehsatà:ke.
Chipmunk — tsitsi. A small striped forest animal; the name is somewhat onomatopoeic of the chipmunk's chattering call.
Squirrel — sakwirá:'a. A tree-dwelling rodent common in the Kanehsatà:ke forests.
Mole — othenón:ton. A burrowing mammal.

Dog — érhar. Dogs have been companion animals to Kanien'kehá:ka people long before European contact. Unlike many domestic animals, the dog has a pre-contact Kanien'kéha name rather than a descriptive post-contact term.
Pig — kwéskwes. The name is somewhat onomatopoeic — kwéskwes echoes the snuffling sounds of a pig. A post-contact animal.
Duck — sísowat. Ducks were both hunted (wild) and domesticated (post-contact). The name captures the duck's sound.
Chicken — á:tarots. A post-contact domestic bird.
Domestic rabbit — distinguished from kwà'ien (wild rabbit) by the tsi tiakowennà:ton (the one that is kept/tame) modifier.
Domestic goose — you know Káhonk (goose) from the birds lesson; the domestic form adds the 'tame' qualifier.
Bullhead — nahòhs. A bottom-feeding fish common in the shallow waters near Kanehsatà:ke.
Catfish — kotihnawá:ries. Related to the salmon root kotihnawá:ra's — both are large river fish.
Minnow — a small fish found throughout the local waterways.
Pigeon or dove — kanón:kwes. A bird common in settled areas.
Loon — variant form. The loon's haunting call on the lake is a defining sound of the Kanehsatà:ke territory at night.
Turkey — skowá:ka. Wild turkey is a traditional food bird. The domestic turkey arrived post-contact. Turkey feathers were also used in regalia.
Snipe — tkaréhton. The snipe bird gives its name to the Snipe Clan (Roti'nehsí:io) present at Kanehsatà:ke.
Forehead — owirá:ke. Upper face vocabulary.
Eyebrow — onón:'a. Related to the eye (Ó:nen) root.
Eyelash — literally 'on the eye'. The locative construction.
Cheek — ohskenón:ton. Note: same spelling as 'arm' in the body parts lesson — this is a different word with the same orthographic form but different stress. Verify with a fluent speaker.
Neck — onhkón:ta.
Shoulder — ohskenón:ni.
Elbow — otshén:ta.
Wrist — ohkwén:te'.
Thumb — onén:ta. Note the connection to -nen- (see) — the thumb's directional quality.
Finger — onén:ta'shon. Extended from the thumb root.
Palm — literally 'the inside of the hand'. You know onenhstà:'a (hand).
Fingernail — onaké:'a.
Body — owirá:'a. The general term for the torso/body.
Leg — otsìtà:'a.
Foot — ohsì:'a. The foot root appears in footwear vocabulary (áhta' = shoe, from the foot root).
Back — ohrá:kwens. Posterior body surface.
Thirteen — ten + three. Óia'k (10) + áhsen (3).
Fourteen — ten + four.
Fifteen — ten + five.
Sixteen — ten + six. Sixteen is also the number of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy founding nations' original settlement sites.
Seventeen — ten + seven.
Eighteen — ten + eight.
Nineteen — ten + nine.
Thirty — three tens. Áhsen (3) + óia'k (10).
Forty — four tens.
Sixty — six tens.
Seventy — seven tens.
Eighty — eight tens.
Ninety — nine tens.
Thimbleberries — shá:ies. A wild berry of the Kanehsatà:ke region, gathered in summer.
Elderberries — orá:se'. Elderberry is also a traditional medicine plant; both the berries and flowers are used.
Plums — wíhshon. Wild plums grew in the Kanehsatà:ke area.
Grapes — ohserón:ni. Wild grapes (not cultivated) grew in the region.
Purple flowering raspberry — a native plant of the Kanehsatà:ke region.
Cantaloupe — a post-contact fruit. Named descriptively as 'the different round thing'.
Carrots — otsì:nekwar nikahtehrò:ten — literally 'yellow (ones) that grow in the ground'. The colour word otsì:nekwahr (yellow) you already know appears in this compound food name.
Onions — tsi oniánhsaton. A descriptive name for the layered bulb.
Cabbage — o'nónkseri.
Beets — onennótakon. Root vegetable.
Green beans — ohsahéta' (beans) + ohontón:tats (growing ones/green). You know ohsahéta' from the Three Sisters. Green beans are young beans harvested before the seeds mature.
Pumpkin — tewátkon. Related to squash (otá:ra) — both are large round gourd vegetables.
Eggs — ohwén:ia. A source of protein in both traditional (wild bird eggs) and modern (chicken eggs) diet.
Rice — a post-contact grain. Named descriptively.
Clams or oysters — shellfish harvested from coastal and river environments.
Turkey meat — skowá:ka (turkey) + o'wá:ron (meat). A compound noun using the compounding pattern you know: animal + o'wá:ron = that animal's meat.
Lobster or crab — ohnhó:nen. Shell-covered crustaceans.
Chicken meat — á:tarots (chicken) + o'wá:ron (meat).
Fish as food — namé:s (fish/trout) + o'wá:ron (meat). You know both components from previous lessons.

The Haudenosaunee headdress — kastowehs. The distinctive upright feather headdress (often called the 'Mohawk headdress') is worn by men at ceremonies. The three eagle feathers stand upright at the back.
Longhouse — alternate spelling/form. You already have kanonsesné:ha. This variant appears in some sources.

The Great Tree of Peace — Kaienrekó:wa. The white pine (ohnhó:ten) that the Peacemaker planted when the Great Law was established. Its roots spread to the four directions, inviting all nations to shelter under it. The eagle sits at the top.
Bow — tekeniteniáhton. The traditional hunting and warfare weapon. The dualic te- prefix in the name reflects the bow's two-part action (drawing and releasing).
Quiver for arrows. Note: distinct from Kaná:tsihs (boat/canoe) — different words with similar forms. The quiver holds the flint arrowheads (Ka'nikonhrhà:wi) you know.

Lacrosse — tewaarathon, literally 'they bump hips' or 'the little brother of war'. Known as the Creator's game (Shonkwaiá:tison onikonhriióhkwa tewaarathon). Played at ceremonies, for healing, and to resolve conflict.
Water drum — katsi'á:wens. A small drum partially filled with water, producing a distinctive resonant sound used in ceremony and social dances.
Ceremonial rattle — okwéskons. Distinct from the toy rattle — the ceremonial rattle accompanies songs and dances.
Corn washing basket — used to wash and process corn. Traditional basket weaving is a living craft at Kanehsatà:ke.
Brooch or pin — kaionhá:ton. Traditional silver brooches are a significant Haudenosaunee art form, particularly in the Kanehsatà:ke and Kahnawà:ke traditions.
Ceremonial pipe — onkwa'nikónhrakon. Like native tobacco (onkwatà:wen), the pipe is used in ceremony to send prayers upward.
Beading loom — kanatsi'tón:nis. The loom used for traditional wampum and decorative beadwork.
Beads — onhkwa'shón:'a. Post-contact glass beads replaced traditional porcupine quills in decorative work. Both arts continue.
Mortar — used with a pestle to grind corn. Traditional corn processing technology.
Buffalo robe — a warm outer garment made from buffalo hide, used in winter. The buffalo (tiotié:ren) you know from the wild animals lesson.
Pastel or soft cloth — a cloth item in the cultural inventory.
Toothbrush — iontwakén:ni. A post-contact hygiene item given a descriptive Kanien'kéha name related to its function (cleaning teeth).
Toothpaste — iontwakén:ni (toothbrush) + onhkwa'a (substance/paste). The compound describes its function.
Bathroom — literally 'the room where one cleans'. A modern household space named descriptively.
Bathtub — kanón:ton. Ka-class household noun.
Face cloth — atié:wen'a. The diminutive form of atié:wen (towel). You already have the towel entry (ho03).
Toilet paper — descriptive compound. You know kahiatónhsera (paper/book) from Ka-class nouns.
Shampoo — ionkwatahonnié:ri. Hair-care product.
Sofa or couch — ohnhó:ra. Household furniture.
Scale — kaniéntha'. A measuring device for weight.
Sky — iotienní:teron. The sky is the domain of the sun, moon, stars, thunderers, and winds — all of which are acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address.
On the earth — ohwén:tsiake. A locative form of ohwén:tsia (earth). Tsi + earth root + ke (locative suffix) = on the earth.

english: on the water / on the lake
notes: On the water — ohnekánoron. Locative expression for being on a body of water. Relevant to canoe travel and fishing.
sortKey: on the water / on the lake

notes: In the water — ohnekáhsonte'. Being inside a body of water. Used in fishing and swimming contexts.
Field, lawn, or meadow — kahentà:ke. Open land. The name Kanehsatà:ke contains a related root — the community name speaks of the land's surface.
Elm — oká:ratsi. The elm bark was traditionally used for longhouse construction. The elm was central to Haudenosaunee architecture before Dutch elm disease devastated the population.
Basswood — ohósera. Basswood fibre was used for traditional rope and baskets.
Cedar — onen'takwenhténtsha. Cedar is one of the four sacred medicines (alongside sweet grass, tobacco, and sage). It is used for purification, protection, and healing.
Birch — okwirà:sen. Birch bark was used for canoe construction and containers.
Hickory — ohsì:ra. Hickory nuts were an important traditional food. Hickory wood is extremely hard and was used for tool handles.
Sunrise — literally 'when the sun rises'. Connects to the ceremonial significance of the east — many ceremonies begin at or face the sunrise.
Sunset — literally 'when the sun goes down'. The west is associated with evening ceremony and reflection in Haudenosaunee tradition.
Daytime — onia'karéhson. The period of sunlight.
Nighttime — iohsiénhaon. The period of darkness. Winter storytelling happens at night.

Thadodaho — Tha'tahon:si. The Onondaga chief whose mind was tangled with evil serpents before the Peacemaker straightened him. He became the fire-keeper of the Confederacy. His name means 'the entangled one' or 'his affairs are in the bushes'.
A longhouse chief — generic term for a male chief of the Confederacy.
The great water serpent — a supernatural creature in Haudenosaunee oral tradition. It lives in deep water and is associated with danger and power.
The Stone Giant — a supernatural creature from Haudenosaunee oral tradition. Stone Giants were cannibalistic beings defeated by Oto:tsa' (a hero figure).

Handsome Lake — Skaniatario (Sganyodaiyo in Seneca). A Seneca prophet (1735–1815) who received visions that became the Longhouse Religion (Gaihwi:io / the Good Word). His Code is still followed in many Haudenosaunee communities.
She makes medicine / medicine woman — iakoiánehr (clan mother form) + tsi oiontátkon (medicine-related). Traditional medicine knowledge was held primarily by women.
She tans hides — iakotsí:sten. Hide-tanning is a traditional women's skill. The -tsí:sten root relates to the tanning process.
A lacrosse player — rakwára:okweh. You know tewaarathon (lacrosse). The player role is named from the game.
Skeleton — ohnharióhkwa. Appears in Haudenosaunee oral tradition as a frightening figure.
The little people — onkwehshón:'a (people) + tsi onnhéhton (small/little). Supernatural small beings in Haudenosaunee tradition who help hunters and know medicine.
The Thunderers — Ratiwéras (they who thunder). Acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address as our grandfathers who bring the spring rains. The sound of thunder is their voices.
Corn husk mask — worn in certain dances and ceremonies. Distinct from the False Face mask.
False Face mask — iotsiskwatáties. Carved wooden masks associated with the False Face Society. Deeply sacred — not displayed for entertainment. Handled only by appropriate knowledge holders.
Fireball — iatkonniákeron. A supernatural light phenomenon in Haudenosaunee oral tradition.
The flying head — oksó:'a. A fearsome supernatural head that flies through the night in some Haudenosaunee legends.
Ghost or spirit — okwire'shón:'a. The spirit of a deceased person who has not yet passed on.
She makes pots — iakoriwá:ienstha. Pottery-making is an ancient Haudenosaunee craft.
Basket makers — ronataierénhthon. Traditional basket weaving continues at Kanehsatà:ke and across Haudenosaunee communities.
Your pants — sa'tarò:ten. sa- possessive prefix + 'tarò:ten (pants root, atháhsteren without the A-class prefix). Compare sahta' (your shoe).
My pants — ka'tarò:ten. ka- + 'tarò:ten.
Your shirt or coat — sakatià:tawi. You know atià:tawi (shirt/coat) from A-class nouns.
My shirt or coat — kakatià:tawi.
Your earrings — sake'wáhshere'. You know ate'wáhshere' (earrings) from A-class nouns.
Your eyeglasses — sake'atkahráhnha'. You know atkahráhnha' (glasses) from A-class nouns.
Your chair — sani'tskwá:ra. You know ani'tskwá:ra (chair).
My chair — kani'tskwá:ra.
Gender-neutral grandchild term. For gendered forms see 'grandson' and 'granddaughter'. The grandparent-grandchild relationship is described as the closest in Haudenosaunee society.
