notes: There — tho. A locative particle indicating a place away from the speaker. Compare: tho niioht (that's right) — literally 'it is there/so'.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

english: now / so then / at this point
notes: Now, so then — onen'. A discourse particle marking a transition or current moment. Appears in Onen' ki' wáhi (that is all) and Onen' ká:tiens (I am now sitting).

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

sortKey: now / so then / at this point

notes: Truly, so it is — wáhi. An affirmative particle expressing truth or completion. Appears in Onen' ki' wáhi and skén:nen ki' wáhi.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: What — standalone form. You have seen this in Nahò:ten katkáhthos? (what do you see?) and Nahò:ten ohén:ton (why). Here as the standalone question word.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Boat or canoe — Kaná:tsihs. You have this in the course. This entry consolidates the primary form.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: First — tsi naió:re. The ordinal 'first' in Kanien'kéha. Used for sequencing: first, second, third.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Second — tékeni tsi naió:re. Literally 'two at the first/order'.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Family, extended family — kahwatsíre'. The broad Kanien'kéha concept of family extends beyond the nuclear family to the entire clan network. Kahwatsíre' is related to -á:tsire' (love/care for).

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Original person / Indigenous person — onkwehón:we. Literally 'real/original person'. The Haudenosaunee term for Indigenous peoples. Compare onkwehshón:'a (the people) in the Thanksgiving Address.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Haudenosaunee — rotinonhsyón:ni, literally 'they are building a longhouse' or 'people who build'. The preferred name for the Iroquois Confederacy in their own languages. Haudenosaunee means the same thing in English (from the Seneca form).

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Lacrosse — tewaarathon. Already in cultural items. This entry provides the standalone form for reference.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: The stars — iotiia'tá:kon. The stars are the last beings acknowledged before the Creator in the Thanksgiving Address. After the moon come the stars, then the Creator.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: School — ionteriwahrónnion. Literally 'the place where matters are discussed / where things are learned'. The language names school by what happens there.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

english: it is good / it is right
notes: It is good — ioiá:ner. The general quality particle for goodness. Related to Kaià:ner (the Good Path / Great Law of Peace). Used as a general expression of approval or correctness.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

sortKey: it is good / it is right

notes: Sweet grass — ohnhónhsaton. One of the four sacred medicines alongside cedar, tobacco, and sage. Braided and burned as a purification offering. Its sweet smoke welcomes positive energy.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Sage — óhserón:ni. One of the four sacred medicines. Burned for purification and protection.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Sky world — otsikonhríhson. The realm above the earth in Haudenosaunee cosmology. The creation story begins here, with Sky Woman falling through a hole in the sky world.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

notes: Sunday — Awentatokenhtí:ke, literally 'the holy/set-apart day'. The day name reflects the influence of Christian missionaries on the Haudenosaunee calendar.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Monday — Awententaón:ke, literally 'the day after the holy day'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Tuesday — Ratironhia'kehronón:ke.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Wednesday — Soséhne.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Thursday — Okarihstiiáhne.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Friday — Ronwaia'tanontaktónhne.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Saturday — Awentákta.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: January — Tsiothorhkó:wa, literally 'the great cold'. The coldest month of winter. Compare kenién:ke (winter) you already know.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: February — Enníska. The month when winter begins to loosen its grip.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: March — Enniskó:wa, 'the great Enniska'. The lengthening days of early spring.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: April — Onerahtókha, 'when the buds open'. The month when trees begin to bud — connected to Sé:ske (spring) and the sugar bush season.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: May — Onerahtohkó:wa, 'the great buds month'. Full spring — planting season begins.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: June — Ohiaríha. You already know Ohiarí:ha as the word for summer (the season). June is the beginning of that season.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: July — Ohyarihkó:wa, 'the great summer'. Peak growing season — corn, beans, and squash are tended.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: August — Seskéha. The month of the Green Corn Ceremony. The corn (onón:ta) begins to be ready for first harvest.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: September — Seskehkó:wa. Full harvest season. The Harvest Ceremony takes place this month.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: October — Kenténha. The leaves turn (Ken'niká:ien = autumn) and harvest concludes.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: November — Kentenhkó:wa. Late autumn; preparation for winter. The Midwinter Ceremony approaches.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: December — Tsiothórha. The beginning of winter (Kenién:ke). Storytelling season begins — stories can only be told when snow is on the ground.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Tree, log, or tree trunk — Karón:ta. A generic tree word. Compare with the specific tree names you already know: wahtá: (maple), oká:ratsi (elm), ohnhó:ten (pine).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Bush, shrub, or whip — Ókwire. The smaller vegetation of the forest understory.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Dirt, dust, or soil — O'kèn:ra. The material of the earth itself. Compare with Onhwéntsia (land/earth as a concept).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Road or street — Oháha. Essential for directions and daily navigation vocabulary.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Rock, stone, or pavement — Ostèn:ra. You know flint (Ka'nikonhrhà:wi) — this is the general term for stone. The Kanien'kehá:ka are the People of the Flint (stone).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Mud — Onawà:tsta. Wet earth. Relevant to spring vocabulary (Sé:ske).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Sand — Onehsarónhkwa. The material of beaches and riverbanks.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: River or stream — Kahiónha. You already have Kaniatarà:ke (the St. Lawrence River specifically). This is the generic river/stream word.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Lake — Kaniá:tara. Used for lakes in general. Kanehsatà:ke is located near the Lake of Two Mountains (Kaniá:tara tsi tékeni karáhkwa).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Rapids — Kah[n]á:wa. The rapids that give Kahnawà:ke its name. 'At the rapids.'

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Swamp, marsh, or wetland — Kaná:wa. The wetlands near Kanehsatà:ke are home to many species including the eel (Aterón:taks) whose population has collapsed.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Fence — Ata'èn:ra. A boundary-marking structure. Post-contact word for the concept of enclosure.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Sky or heaven — Karonhià:ke. Literally 'in the sky'. The sky world (otsikonhríhson) of Haudenosaunee cosmology — where Sky Woman lived before falling through the hole.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Snow — Óniehte. The presence of snow on the ground marks the storytelling season. Kanehsatà:ke — 'where the crust of snow on the earth is' — takes its name from this substance.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Field or garden — Kahéhta. The cultivated land where the Three Sisters grow. You already know kahentà:ke (field/meadow) — this is the general term.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Clay, pottery, or chimney — O'tá:ra. Clay is the material of traditional Haudenosaunee pottery. The same root names both the material and the objects made from it.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: At the school — tsi yontaweya'táhkwa. The locative phrase for school. You already have ionteriwahrónnion (school as an institution) — this is the directional/locative form used when saying 'going to school'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Library — tsi yehyatonhserayentáhkwa, literally 'the place where writings are kept'. You know kahiatónhsera (paper/book) — the library is where books are stored.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Mall or large store — tsi yontenhninon'tha'kó:wa, 'the big place where things are bought'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Band office or community office — tsi thatitsyénhayens. The administrative centre of a First Nations community.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Radio station — tsi waterennótha. CKRK (91.9 FM) is Kahnawà:ke's Kanien'kéha language radio station. CKON broadcasts from Akwesasne. Radio is a key Kanien'kéha language domain.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Hospital — tsi tyakenheyontayentáhkwa, 'the place where people are healed'. An important modern vocabulary item.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: At the longhouse — kanonhséhsne. The locative form of Kanonsesné:ha (longhouse). Used when saying 'I am going to the longhouse'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Restaurant — tsi teyontska'hónhkwa, 'the place where one eats'. The eating verb root tsk- you know from sátskhwa' (you eat).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Six Nations Grand River Territory — Ohswé:ken. Home of the largest population of Haudenosaunee people, located near Brantford, Ontario. The Six Nations Polytechnic that produced the eCampusOntario OER is located here.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Montréal — Tyohtyà:ke (or Tiohtià:ke in some orthographies), 'where the group split'. Kanehsatà:ke is located approximately 60km west of Montréal. The city's Kanien'kéha name refers to a historical division point.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Toronto — Tsi tkarón:to, 'where trees stand in water'. The Kanien'kéha name for the city refers to the fish weirs or trees that once stood in the bay.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Ottawa — Kanà:tsyo. The capital of Canada, named from an Algonquian word; this is the Kanien'kéha form.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Ant — tsi'nhontstókhi. The industrious ant appears in Haudenosaunee teaching stories.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Honey bee — katsikhe'tón:nis. Bees and their honey are referenced in food vocabulary. The honey bee is a post-contact introduction to North America — the Haudenosaunee called it 'the white man's fly'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Butterfly — tsiktsinón:nawen. Butterflies appear in oral tradition as symbols of transformation.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Firefly — tewatsirókwas, 'the one that flashes twice'. The te- dualic prefix marks the two-phase flash pattern of the firefly.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Fly — tsiks. One of the shortest words in Kanien'kéha vocabulary. Flies are part of the natural world acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Frog (or amphibian more generally) — tsikerentányaks.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Mosquito — okaryahtà:ne. A familiar presence in the Kanehsatà:ke wetlands.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Spider — takwa'áhson. Spiders appear in Haudenosaunee oral tradition as weavers and trap-makers.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Tick — ohséhton. A forest parasite relevant to health vocabulary in the Kanehsatà:ke woodland territory.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Worm — otsi'nonwanhè:ta. Earthworms are connected to the soil and composting vocabulary.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Mouse — tsinó:wen. Compare tsinowen'kó:wa (rat = big mouse).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Rat — tsinowen'kó:wa, literally 'big mouse'. You know tsinó:wen (mouse) — the augmentative -kó:wa makes it the large version.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Opossum — waniheyà:tha. North America's only marsupial. Its playing-dead defence strategy is referenced in some oral tradition.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Otter — tawí:ne (Six Nations form). You have enia'tarón:nis in the glossary from Kontinónhstats — this is the variant form from the eCampusOntario OER, representing a different dialect.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Jackrabbit — tehahonhtané:ken. A larger hare species, distinct from kwà'ien (wild rabbit).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Moose — ska'nyónhsa (Six Nations dialect form). You have tsihnón:saron from Kontinónhstats. This variant from eCampusOntario represents the Six Nations/Tyendinaga dialect.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Raccoon — atíhron. The raccoon's masked face is referenced in oral tradition and children's stories.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Cougar or lynx — kén:reks. A large wild cat of the northeastern forests. Rarely seen today in the Kanehsatà:ke territory but historically present.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Chickadee — tsiktsiré:re. The name is onomatopoeic — it echoes the chickadee's call. One of the most common year-round birds in the Kanehsatà:ke region.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Crow — ká:ka. Crows are intelligent birds prominent in Haudenosaunee oral tradition. The crow's call warns of danger.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Duck — so:ra (Six Nations form). You have sísowat from Kontinónhstats. This is the variant form from the eCampusOntario OER.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Grouse — ohkwé:sen. A ground-nesting bird hunted in the forests around Kanehsatà:ke. The ruffed grouse is common in the region.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Hummingbird — raónraon. The name may be onomatopoeic of the hummingbird's wing sound. Hummingbirds arrive in May and depart in September.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Killdeer or sandpiper — tawístawis. Also the name of one of the four Kanehsatà:ke clan animal symbols shown on the kanienkeha.org site.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Loon — tsyonya'tarèn:ton (Six Nations form). You have Ohkwá:ri kahón: in the glossary. This is the variant from the eCampusOntario OER.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Woodpecker — karontároks, 'the one that pecks/knocks on trees'. The root karón:ta (tree) you just learned + the knocking action.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Cardinal — kanekwenhtárha. The brilliant red male cardinal is a striking winter presence in the Kanehsatà:ke region.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Robin — tsiskó:ko (Six Nations form). You have Kwáwenhs from Kontinónhstats. This variant from the eCampusOntario OER.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Swan — onahsakèn:ra. White swans migrate through the Lake of Two Mountains area near Kanehsatà:ke.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I love him — ri- (I→him) + nó:ronhkwa (love root). The root -nó:ronhkwa is the love/affection root. Compare konwá:tsire' (I love her) which you already know. Different prefixes for different relationships.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Work verb. kate- (I, stative) + rí:waks; ra- (he) + í:waks. Essential for daily conversation.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am learning — kate- (I) + rí:henta' (learning root). An important self-referential verb for language learners. 'Kanien'kéha katerí:henta'' = I am learning Kanien'kéha.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Two related speaking roots: ná:ton (to say/speak) and -wenna- (word-making). The root -wenna- is related to words and language — compare onkwawenna (our language).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I write — ka- (I) + hiá:ton (writing root) + -s (habitual). You know kahiatónhsera (paper/book = the writing-thing) — this is the verb from which that noun is derived.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: You swim — s- (you) + athahkwatáhkwa's (swimming root + habitual). An important recreational and practical verb for a community on the water.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am cooking — kate- (I) + ronní:io (cooking root). You know satorá:wen (you cook, command) — this is the habitual 'I am cooking' form.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am sick — katonnhétons. A key health vocabulary verb.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is raining — tewatkahráthos. The weather verb for rain. The te- dualic prefix suggests rain as a two-phase action (falling + landing).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is snowing — tewatníehton. The weather verb for snow. You know Óniehte (snow, noun).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is windy — iokahrónhkwa. The wind (Orénta) vocabulary you know from nature, expressed as a verb state.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Okay then — Ha' ki'. Ha' = okay/well; ki' = just/only. Together a soft affirmative transition: 'okay then, let's go', 'alright then'. Appears frequently in natural speech.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: New or newly — asé'tsi. A quality particle. 'Áhsirasé'tsi ne thi' = that is a new blanket (from the Deering & Harries-Delisle wordlist).

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

english: it's finished / it's done / that's it
notes: It is finished — ón:ne'. Marks completion of an action or situation. Compare Onen' ki' wáhi (that is all, speech closing) — both mark endings.

cite.text: Kanehsatà:ke community usage; Maracle (1993) One Thousand Useful Mohawk Words

sortKey: it's finished / it's done / that's it

notes: My cousin — onkyara'sé:'a. In Haudenosaunee society, cousins of the same clan are considered more like siblings. Cross-clan cousins are the appropriate marriage partners.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: My friend (male) — onkyatén:ron. Friendship in Kanien'kéha is expressed using gendered terms. The eCampusOntario OER shows that 'friend' changes based on the gender of the friend.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: My uncle — rakenohá:'a (maternal uncle, from a male speaker). The maternal uncle has a particular responsibility in Haudenosaunee society — teaching his sister's children. You had rakhén:ha earlier; this is an alternate form.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: My older siblings (plural) — akhtsì:son. The collective form of the older sibling term. When multiple siblings are referred to together.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Blanket — áhsire'. An A-class noun. 'Áhsirasé'tsi ne thi' = that is a new blanket. Blankets are significant both as everyday objects and as ceremonial gifts.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Table — atekhwá:ra'. An A-class household noun. 'Atekhwá:ra ken ne thi?' = Is that a table?

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Axe — ato:ken'. A traditional and post-contact tool. The axe root appears in compound tool names.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Spoon — atókwa'. A kitchen utensil. The verb for eating (sátskhwa') shares a root with the spoon.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Gloves or mittens — a'niá:nawen. Winter handwear, relevant to cold-weather vocabulary.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Hat or cap — anón:warore'. A post-contact head covering. The word possibly relates to the turtle shell (a'nó:wara = turtle) due to the dome shape.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Mirror — atáthken'. A post-contact reflective object given a descriptive Kanien'kéha name.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Pail or container — aón:ta'. A general container noun, useful for kitchen and outdoor vocabulary.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Bracelet — atenen'tsháhna'. Silver bracelets are a significant Haudenosaunee art form, closely associated with the Kanehsatà:ke and Kahnawà:ke silversmithing tradition.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Friendship — atenró:sera. An abstract noun for the quality of friendship. Related to the friend terms (onkyatén:ron etc.) — the same root.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Riverside, on the edge of a river — atsá:kta. A locative noun for being beside a body of water. Kanehsatà:ke's geography makes this a common expression.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Clothing — atsherón:nia'. The collective term for all clothing. Compare atahkwénnia' (clothes, another collective form). You know individual clothing items; this groups them.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: A day — enhnísera. The unit of a day. Compare onia'karéhson (daytime) — enhnísera is more the countable unit ('three days = áhsen enhnísera').

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Tomorrow — eniorhén'ne. An essential daily planning word. 'Eniorhén'ne ohron'ké:ne' = tomorrow morning.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Tonight — enió:karahwe'. 'Enió:karahwe' thoién:ke' = I'll go there tonight.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: In the morning — ohron'ké:ne. Compare tsi ká:nenhste' (another form for morning). Both are used in time expressions.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: At midnight — ahsón'then. The exact middle of the night.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: At noon — ón:tie'. The exact middle of the day.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Paddle — aká:we'. The canoe paddle. You know Kaná:tsihs (canoe/boat) — now you have the paddle that drives it.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Lover or romantic partner — ákskere'. A term for a romantic companion. Related to the friend and companion vocabulary.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Fresh fruit — á:iase'. A general term for fresh/raw fruit as opposed to dried or processed fruit.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Beer or fermented drink — onontákeri. 'Enhsnekíra ken onontákeri?' = Will you drink a beer? A post-contact item.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Coffee — káwhe. 'Enhsnekíra ken káwhe?' = Will you drink a coffee? A post-contact drink.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

notes: Dollar(s) — nikawístake. 'Ahsen nikawístake' = three dollars. The money-counting expression. You know Ohwísta (money) — this is the unit used in counting amounts.

cite.text: Deering & Harries-Delisle, Mohawk: A Teaching Grammar (via kanienkeha.org)

english: it is good / it is beautiful
notes: It is good / beautiful — kenní:io. A general positive quality stative verb. One of the most commonly heard expressions of approval.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

sortKey: it is good / it is beautiful

notes: It is new or fresh — asé:tsion. Compare asé'tsi from Deering & Harries-Delisle. A quality stative verb.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is old or ancient — ratón:kwe. Used of objects and situations, not people (for old people you have Rokstén:ha / Akokstén:ha).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is expensive — iowen'niáton. A useful shopping and commerce vocabulary item.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is cheap or inexpensive — io'tiesénha. The complement to expensive. Useful in market and shopping contexts.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Fin — okawè:tshera. Fish anatomy vocabulary useful for fishing and food preparation contexts.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Gill — watonryéhtha. Fish anatomy. The gill is how fish breathe underwater.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Scale — ótsta. Fish scales. You know the verb for removing scales (tsí'stenht) from the I-stem verb lesson.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Feather (generic) — ohstò:sera. You already have Ohón:ta (feather/feathers) in the glossary. This is the more specific anatomical term for a single feather.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Wing — onerahóntsha. Bird wing anatomy. The wing root appears in flight and flying verbs.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am afraid — Wakhterón:ni. An emotional state verb. The wak- prefix marks first-person stative. The root -terón:ni expresses fear.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am angry — Wakena'khwén:'on. The root -na'khwén:'on expresses anger. Awareness of emotional vocabulary is central to LaFever's emotional domain of learning.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am busy — Tewakeweiénhare. The te- dualic prefix suggests the 'split attention' of being busy.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: i am content / I feel good (inside)
notes: I am content, I feel good inside — Wake'nikonhrí:io. The -nikonhr- root is the mind/heart root you know from Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne. -í:io means good. Literally: my mind/heart is good.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

sortKey: i am content / i feel good (inside)

notes: I am depressed or heavy-hearted — Wake'nikonhrén:ton. The -nikonhr- (mind/heart) root + -én:ton (heavy/laden). Literally: my heart/mind is heavy.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am happy — Wakatshennón:ni. One of the most important emotional state verbs. The -tshennón:ni root expresses happiness and celebration.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am sad — Wake'nikonhráksen. Mind/heart (-nikonhr-) + dark/bad (-áksen). Literally: my heart is darkened.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

english: i have time / I am free
notes: I have time — Wakenáktote. A useful practical phrase for making plans and social arrangements.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

sortKey: i have time / i am free

notes: I am waiting — Wakerhá:re. Essential for everyday situations: waiting for someone, waiting in line.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am lucky — Wakatera'swí:io. Luck/fortune as a state of being.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am unlucky — Wakatera'swáksen. The complement to being lucky. -áksen (dark/bad) suffix you saw in 'I am sad'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am smart or clever — Kattókha.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am lazy — Kentórha. One of those honestly useful everyday state verbs.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am ready or prepared — Wakatateweiennentà:'on. An important practical phrase before starting activities.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am thrilled or excited — Wakatonháhere.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am surprised — Wakenehrákwas.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am mean or nasty — Ikhseróhen. A state verb for negative interpersonal disposition.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am lonesome or lonely — Kentón:nis. An important emotional state, particularly relevant to language revitalization conversations about cultural isolation.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am worried or concerned — Tewake'nikonhrhare. Mind/heart (-nikonhr-) + worried. The te- dualic marks divided/troubled attention.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is dangerous — Ióthteron. An important safety and warning vocabulary item.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am funny — Wakhsté:rihst. Compare Iohsté:rihst (it is funny — impersonal). The same root with different prefixes.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am aware, I know — Wakaterién:tare. Compare Kienté:ri (I know something specific). Awareness vs specific knowledge are distinguished.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I know something specific — Kienté:ri. Compare Wakaterién:tare (general awareness). The distinction between knowing-in-general and knowing-a-specific-thing is grammatically marked.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: When? — Kátke. The question word for time. You had Nahò:ten iohrhó:ten as a when-expression from L11; this is the shorter direct question word from the eCampusOntario OER.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: How many? — To nihá:ti. The quantity question word. Appears in counting contexts: 'To nihá:ti ratiksa'okón:'a?' (how many boys?).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Why? — Oh nontyé:ren. Alternate form from the eCampusOntario question words chapter. You have Nahò:ten ohén:ton (why?) from L11 — this is a variant used in Six Nations dialect.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: I am hungry — Katonhkaria'ks. ka- (I) + tonhkaria'ks (hungry root). A body-state verb relating to food.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Food (ready-to-eat) — kákhwa. A general term for prepared, ready-to-eat food. Compare atennà:tshera (groceries/lunch).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Fruit — káhik. A general term for fruit. Compare the specific fruit names you already know.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Groceries or lunch — atennà:tshera. A food-parcel noun. Also appears in kinship vocabulary (akotenná:tshera' = her food).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Bread — owihstóhsera. The general bread term. You have serihwahsótha (you make bread, fake E-stem verb) — this is the noun.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Squash — onon'ónsera. You know Otá:ra (squash, one of the Three Sisters) from unit 7. This is a variant/generic form from the eCampusOntario Six Nations dialect.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Potato — ohnennà:ta. A post-contact root vegetable that became central to Haudenosaunee and broader northeastern diets. Its Kanien'kéha name is descriptive.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Soup — onon'ónsera súkhar (or onon'ónsera alone in some dialects). You know onenhstóhkwa (corn soup specifically). This is the general soup word.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Maple syrup — wahtá: (maple tree, which you know) + óhses (sap/syrup). The product of the sugar bush. A central food and ceremony item.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is delicious — yawékon. A food quality stative verb. Used in the eating conversation: 'Iawekon ken?' (is it delicious?)

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is cooked or done — yó:ri. A food state verb. 'Iah teio:ri ki:ken ___' = this food is not cooked all the way.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is raw or uncooked — okáhte. The complement to yó:ri (cooked). Food safety and preparation vocabulary.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is hot (temperature) — yo'taríhen. Applied to food or drink that is hot to the touch/taste. Compare iotontátsion (it is hot weather).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is cool or cold — yowíhsto. Applied to cold food or drink. Compare ionkwahró:ri (cold weather).

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is burnt — yotsha'áhton. 'Iotshon ki:ken ___' = this food is burnt. A food quality warning.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is boiled — teyohnekontyéhton. Boiling is a primary traditional cooking method. The te- dualic reflects the two-phase process.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is fried or deep-fried — kakerì:ten. Post-contact cooking method named descriptively.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is greasy — yawenhyé:nare. A food texture/quality description.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: It is spicy or hot-tasting — teyotskara'kó:wa. Distinguishes temperature-hot (yo'taríhen) from spice-hot.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Strawberry — niyohontéhsha (Six Nations OER form). You have Ken' niiohontésha' from Kontinónhstats. This Six Nations variant represents the dialect variation across communities.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Apple — sewahyó:wane. You have Kakhwá:yen (apple) from Kontinónhstats. This is the alternate Six Nations form. Both are correct in their respective dialects.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Maple syrup/sap — wáhta (maple) + óhses (flows/drips). The sap that flows from the maple tree in spring. The Sugar Bush (Tsi Wahta' Iontewahronkón:ni) is the ceremonial gathering around this flow.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Beer — kahwatstón:ni, literally 'the foamy thing'. Compare onontákeri (beer, Deering form). Dialect variation in naming post-contact substances.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Garlic — teyokháhon. A flavouring plant used in cooking.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Oatmeal, cornmush, or pudding — otsíhskwa. A cooked grain or corn dish. Cornmush (otsíhskwa made with onón:ta) is a traditional Haudenosaunee food.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Ice cream — kanennyo'kóhare, literally 'frozen snow food'. The root -nennyo'kw- = frozen snow. A descriptive post-contact food name.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Corn soup — onenhstóhkwa. You know onón:ta (corn) and onon:tara (soup). Corn soup is a central traditional Haudenosaunee dish, made from dried white corn treated with hardwood ash lye.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Sausage, hot dog, or baloney — awenhétsha. Post-contact processed meat.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Fried bread or bannock — tekarihstó:yen, 'stove-top bread'. The root -rihst- = stove. Fried bread became prevalent after contact and is now a significant community food, though its origins are tied to colonialism and food displacement.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Cranberry — tó:kwahre. A wild berry traditionally harvested in bogs and wetlands near Kanehsatà:ke.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Raspberries — skanekwenhtará:yen. A wild berry gathered in summer.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Watermelon — wahyá:ris. A post-contact fruit given a Kanien'kéha name.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Blackberry — shá:yehse. A wild berry of the forests and field edges.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Blueberry — karha'takéha, 'forest one'. The -rha- root = forest (kárha), so blueberry = 'the one from the forest'.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Cherry — é:ri. Wild cherries were gathered traditionally. The augmented form eri'kó:wa = big cherry.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Plum — wíhson káhik (OER form, 'plum fruit'). You have wíhshon from Kontinónhstats. This compound form clarifies it as a fruit category.

cite.text: eCampusOntario OER — Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic

notes: Dark red — Kanekwenhtarahòn:tsi. The -hòn:tsi suffix marks darkness/depth of colour.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Light red — Kanekwenhtararà:ken. The -rà:ken suffix marks lightness of colour.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Orange — Akiarèn:ta. Six Nations Polytechnic OER form.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Light green — Kahontarà:ken. Light variant of Óhonte (green).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Dark blue — Karonhiahòn:tsi. Dark variant of Oròn:ia (blue).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Light blue — Karonhiarà:ken. Light variant of Oròn:ia (blue).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Purple — Oharennáhta. OER form; compare Ariwa'konhné:ha (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Mauve / light purple — Waharennahtarà:ken. Light variant of Oharennáhta.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Pink — Wahsén:rate. OER form; compare Washén:ra (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Pale pink — Wahsenrate'tsherarà:ken. Light variant of Wahsén:rate.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Tan or light brown — Watehsarà:ken. Light variant of Athéhsa (brown).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Black — Kahòn:tsi. OER form; compare Kayé:ri (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Grey — Ata'kenhróhkwa. OER form; compare Oseráhne (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Light grey — Wata'kenhrohkwarà:ken. Light variant of Ata'kenhróhkwa.

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: White — Karà:ken. OER form; compare Ó:wira' (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: One — Énskat (OER / Six Nations form). Compare Énska (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Nine — Kióhton (OER form). Compare Tióhton (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Ten — Oié:ri (OER form). Compare Óia'k (Kontinónhstats).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Hundred number marker — Tewen'nyá:we. Used when expressing hundreds (e.g., Tewen'nyá:we + number = that many hundreds).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Teens marker — iawén:re. Added after the base number to form 11–19 (e.g., Énskat iawén:re = eleven).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Tens marker — niwáhsen. Used to form multiples of ten (e.g., Áhsen niwáhsen = thirty).

cite.text: Mohawk Language Learning Resources (CC BY 4.0) · Six Nations Polytechnic · Stevie Jonathan, Tehakanere Henhawk, Jersey Squire

notes: Gender-neutral child — Eksá:'a. Distinct from gendered Raksá:'a (boy) and Yeksá:'a (girl) in the OER.

english: to take shade from the sun

english: the pet I used to have

english: they will go and get him back
sortKey: they will go and get him back

notes: Three uses: (1) a female animal, (2) a familiar female — sister or close friend, (3) an inanimate object. Familiarity and animacy are encoded grammatically in Kanien'kéha rather than through separate pronouns.
First person dual inclusive. The teiaken- prefix marks 'you and I together'. Distinct from teniáhshe (us two, exclusive — not including the listener).
Dual pronoun for two females. The tete- prefix marks a feminine dual. Compare with tehniiáhshe (two males or mixed pair).
For two males, or one male and one female together. The teh- prefix marks a masculine or mixed dual.
First person plural inclusive — all of us, including the person being spoken to. Distinct from konwé:kon (all females, exclusive) and ratiié:ren (all males or mixed plural).
Third person feminine plural. Used when referring to a group of women or girls. The kon- prefix marks feminine plural.
Third person masculine/mixed plural. Can mean 'all the males' or, in context, 'everybody' (inclusive of males). The ra- prefix marks masculine; -tié:ren marks plural.
Your daughter. The she- prefix marks second person possession (your). Daughter and son forms use distinct prefix patterns from sibling forms.
Your son. The she- prefix marks second person possession (your). Compare with Akhsotá (my grandmother) — kinship terms in Kanien'kéha encode the relationship from the speaker's perspective.
A simpler form of Niawenhkó:wa (thank you greatly). Niawén: is the root; -kwe is a softening particle. Used in everyday contexts; Niawenhkó:wa is more formal or emphatic.
The most common everyday greeting. Shé:kon also means 'still' or 'yet' in non-greeting contexts — the greeting use is idiomatic. Returned in kind: if someone says Shé:kon, you reply Shé:kon.

Wá:s — 'Go!' Command form for one person. Kanien'kéha verbs encode person, number, gender, and aspect. Structure: pronominal prefix + verb root + aspect suffix. For plural command (go, all of you), the prefix changes.
